title: A look at Cairn (indie RPG) along with the Escape the City module
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fKvvShsdaWU

Daniel (@dungeondive) pairs Yochai Gao's rules-light indie RPG *Cairn* with the *Escape the City* solo module to demonstrate one of his go-to combinations for getting into solo play. Cairn itself is short and narrative-leaning: three stats (Strength, Dexterity, Will), roll-under-d20 tests, no levels, an inventory-slot system where fatigue eats slots, and Hit Protection that — when reduced to exactly zero — sends you to the Scars table for narrative consequences. Combat is fast and deadly. Daniel rates it as a good first-step indie RPG because the book front-loads guidance for both Warden and players.

*Escape the City* takes a standard deck of cards split into red (locations) and black (encounters), and tasks you with finding three secrets in one cardinal direction to identify and overcome the bridge guard blocking your escape from the zealot-occupied island city of Otawu. Daniel's headline note: don't approach it as a Four Against Darkness-style crawl. The encounters set up roleplay scenarios — light journalling makes the adventure memorable, and the oracles reward you for treating the city as a sandbox rather than a flowchart.

----

Cairn and Escape the City both lean on the player to do the interpretive work — is that the right pairing for someone genuinely new to solo, or does rules-light demand experience to fill the gaps?
